posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Having been drinking Ridge since the 1991 vintage, I feel like 2013 is when they jumped the shark. Pretty much every wine I’ve had from that vintage - and that’s many, from the Zins to the Cabs - have been way over-oaked. Perhaps Geyserville is the exception. I do like that Geezer.

I was super excited to try a Cab Franc from Monte Bello Vineyards. Some of you know that I’m a Loire Cab Franc fan. It’s not that I expect New World CF to taste like Loire, but sheez, I certainly don’t want my CF to taste like vanilla and dill. The pronounced American Oak obscures the wonderful nuances, and savory and herbal qualities, that CF has. I will say, there is a delicious sexy quality to this wine but it’s like one glass max, then way to OTT for my clunky palate. Gorgeous fruit enveloped in oak. I just checked the winery website, it’s 100% new oak for 20 mos. Why why why?

I’m not even sure how to rate a wine like this. I don’t want to drink it.

Paired this with a gorgeous, 28 ounce Flannery Jorge steak - in homage to our moderators - reverse seared medium-rare on the rare side. I moved on to a Levet that I popped last night.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
Great thread on (for me) the bummer topic of my favorite American winery changing styles.

Like many of us, Geyserville is my favorite Ridge wine although I’ve had many truly spectacular Ridge cabs from the 70s and 80’s. When Ridge stopped making cabs from York Creek and Jimsomare, they lost something for my tastes that their new cabs (Klein, Torre, and others) didn’t replace. And I haven’t really found Monte Bello to be revelatory, as it never seems ready and/or it is overoaked.

My simple math is — non-bordeaux varietals stand up much better to the American oak, and Geyserville is my fav zin-based Ridge, so I buy that (though reports that the 18 Geezer is OTT are scary). My last cabs from Ridge are from 2013, apropos this thread.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I thought they have used 100% American oak forever. Has the char changed?

On which wine?

The percentage varies on many of the wines. And yes, some, like MB, tend to be 100%.

Just checked on the website, and for the Rousten Cab Franc, 2013 and 2017 are 100% new and 2016 is 60% new. The website shows no other vintages of this wine.

By comparison, the 2013 Estate Cab is 48% new and 42% one year. The 2013 Monte Bello is 100% new. Geyserville is 20% new with only 12 months in barrel.

According to the tours I’ve had at Ridge and various blogs, Draper “shared” wine making duties with Eric Baugher for years, as he tapered his involvement. Here’s an official timeline: “Over the years as I stepped back from day to day operations, I named Eric winemaker for Monte Bello in 2001.” Draper retired officially in 2016.

Not a lot to parse there in terms of obvious changes in leadership and changes in oak.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
The discussions of Ridge and oak usage are always interesting - with many defending the winery and claiming that the wines do not show exuberant amounts of oak and others who are dismayed.

I think that as others have said, American oak has its place in their line up. It seems to me that in their lineup, it works well with Monte Bello, their other cab, their zins and their PS. When it comes to other rhones and, apparently cab franc, it sticks out like a sore thumb . . .

Cheers.

As someone whose tasting experience with young Ridge wines goes back to the mid-'90s, I do not see a stylistic change from '91 on. There are bigger vintage variations than practically any other CA winery, with ABV of a wine varying by 3% and the makeup of blends being quite different from one year to the next. That spurs Chicken Little threads now and then. People did that with the '01 Monte Bello, which is still, by far, the highest ABV vintage, assuming it marked some tragic stylistic shift. No, just picking to make the best wine in an unusual vintage. (Just had one Friday. The youthful exuberance is gone, which atypically I’d loved on that wine, and it’s on its way half-way to its peak. First Growth CA from a great vintage.)

Sure, American oak is a signature. Their Rhones stand out as something different. I think part of some peoples’ issue is they don’t understand where the peak on Ridge wine is, so they drink them too young, the oak hasn’t integrated, so they think it won’t. Try a '90s era Syrah. It has. Others are just hyper-sensitive to American oak and complain about it standing out in '70s era Monte Bello. Then why jump into theads to complain over and over? I’ve suggested several times they try Montebello ridge Cabs from other excellent producers (Arnot Roberts, I. Brand…) who use French oak, but don’t see notes on those. There’s also the nearby Mount Eden Vineyard. The Mount Eden Cab is better than ever, and the separately trained and managed Peter Martin Ray blocks are shining (the '17 Birichino and '16 Ceritas are coming around and showing their world-class). I hear there are plenty of other Zins, Rhones, Chards, etc. being made in CA.

posts.cooked:
We’ve had this discussion before, lol:

WineBerserkers – 24 Dec 20

Dunn Howell Mountain vs Ridge Montebello

Admittedly, this may be a odd comparison. However, I found myself in a conversation with two others who were extolling the virtues of waiting for structured, tannic California cabernets to mature. Each of the two had only tasted one of the wines...

Reading time: 18 mins 🕑 Likes: 18 ❤

Klein ≈ Jimsomare, but not quite (according to online info). How closely they match, I don’t know, it depends on how much of the (best) formerly-Jimsomare fruit Ridge is putting into the Monte Bello Historic Vines, Torre Ranch, and Steep Terraces bottlings.

Klein has never really tasted for me like a Jimsomare cab — it seems bigger, oaker, riper. Still very good, and to be fair I never tried a Jimsomare cab with less than 12 years of age. Jury is out IMHO if these Klein’s will morph into something more elegant a la the Jimsomares of old. But I usually don’t bet against the aging ability of Ridge wines!

Just checked on the website, and for the Rousten Cab Franc, 2013 and 2017 are 100% new and 2016 is 60% new. The website shows no other vintages of this wine.

That makes a lot of sense. I was going to say that I opened the first of my 2016 last year, and it didn’t seem out of balance as far as oak is concerned.

I don’t have a 2013 to compare, but will likely try a 2010 soon-ish. Vines were young at a the time, not sure if/how that factored into the oak treatment then. My perception is that the overall style has not changed drastically in the Draper/Baugher transition period. There certainly is variation from vintage to vintage of course.

Rousten is part of their Historic Vineyard Series. That’s for parcels that don’t make the Monte Bello cut, but show distinct and high quality, so get bottled as a SVD rather than going into the Estate. The oak decision is made months before the assemblage process begins, so it’s more a best guess for what each lot that looks like it could make Monte Bello needs to best contribute to that blend, rather than aiming to make it as a stand alone. None of these is made every year.

As far as '13 goes, my early impression was this would be a longer than typical ager. It shouldn’t be a surprise they’re made so the oak integrates near peak. Even if that were a poor vintage, peak is a long way off.

I stick with my “don’t even think about opening this until” rule. 10 years for lesser reds and Chard. 20 years for most others. 30 years for Monte Bello. Adjust upward.
Or, interpret the back label guidance as follows: “Drinks well now. Should continue to improve over the next 8 to 12 years.” means don’t think about opening your first bottle for at least 12 years.

posts.name: Allan Bree
posts.cooked: The term “Draper perfume” was coined by geo t more than twenty years ago and was used to describe the characteristic aromatic profile of the Ridge wines from the 90s and earlier. It was never intended to describe a particular oak regimen nor to suggest a preponderance of wood in the aromatics and flavors of the wines.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
As someone whose tasting experience with young Ridge wines goes back to the mid-'90s, I do not see a stylistic change from '91 on. There are bigger vintage variations than practically any other CA winery, with ABV of a wine varying by 3% and the makeup of blends being quite different from one year to the next. That spurs Chicken Little threads now and then. People did that with the '01 Monte Bello, which is still, by far, the highest ABV vintage, assuming it marked some tragic stylistic shift. No, just picking to make the best wine in an unusual vintage. (Just had one Friday. The youthful exuberance is gone, which atypically I’d loved on that wine, and it’s on its way half-way to its peak. First Growth CA from a great vintage.)

Sure, American oak is a signature. Their Rhones stand out as something different. I think part of some peoples’ issue is they don’t understand where the peak on Ridge wine is, so they drink them too young, the oak hasn’t integrated, so they think it won’t. Try a '90s era Syrah. It has. Others are just hyper-sensitive to American oak and complain about it standing out in '70s era Monte Bello. Then why jump into theads to complain over and over? I’ve suggested several times they try Montebello ridge Cabs from other excellent producers (Arnot Roberts, I. Brand…) who use French oak, but don’t see notes on those. There’s also the nearby Mount Eden Vineyard. The Mount Eden Cab is better than ever, and the separately trained and managed Peter Martin Ray blocks are shining (the '17 Birichino and '16 Ceritas are coming around and showing their world-class). I hear there are plenty of other Zins, Rhones, Chards, etc. being made in CA.

There is a lot of really good stuff packed into this comment, thank you. I love the Mount Eden wines. I have been meeting to try the I. Brand wines, but the two times that I’ve reached out to them, I got no response. That said, I do not understand your criticism of the criticism. If all we did on this website is applaud wineries and never criticize, it would be a very boring website. Had somebody like a Pat Martin posted a note on the wine I just popped, or told me that the oak signature of obscured the varietal, I never would’ve spent $95 on this one wine. Criticism and opinions, negative or positive, are very important to the very lifeblood of this website. That the criticism is over a legendary American winery and winemaker, or a board favorite, should not matter.

My criticism is that, while Ridge is a board favorite, the continued pounding from people complaining about the oak has worn out the fans, so they don’t bother replying in yet another thread on the same same same topic. That leads to a very skewed perception of a (small) minority opinion being the dominant opinion. So, it’s about weird obsession and endurance.

The seven CT notes on this wine are a love fest. I’d agree a note in the mix there with a dissenting view would be helpful.

Totally disagree.

You could posit the exact opposite, that dissenters are afraid to dissent because of this, and being contrarian. I think we should call balls and strikes, and not be afraid to say it. Not many notes here on this Cab Franc, I’m not pulling punches. I’m not ITB.

I noticed I was even moderating myself recently on a wine I like very much, the Halcon Alturas. I was not thrilled with the 2019. Sure it’s a baby and sure it needs time, but this was a much larger-scaled wine that my normal experience with them. Your reference to CT doesn’t really resonate with me, those are normally glowing. I did see Carnes’ note on this Halcon, he was spot on, but kind, reading between the lines.

I bet if you tallied up the posts on Ridge, they are 10:1 positive or better.

posts.cooked: Obviously, oak is a controversial subject. To my taste, a wine that tastes of new oak won’t lose that flavor when it ages, though its place in the flavor profile may change. I may be wrong about that with some wines and I certainly haven’t tasted as many wines, both young and old, as some here have, but that is my experience. It is also my experience with Ridge Zins dating back to the 90s. As I said, I have become less oak tolerant as I have aged and that has lessened my appreciation of Ridge Zins. I’m with Robert in the idea that this board is here, in part, to register individuals’ tastings of wines. They will differ for the same wines for any number of reasons, including one’s tolerance for oak. I am also aware that people who are fans of a wine don’t like to see negative tasting notes for that wine and will naturally try to explain that in terms of flawed bottles or not knowing how the wine will age or, most objectionably, bias because of the opinions of others. I think those people need to remember one basic truth: people are different.

posts.name: Eric Baugher
posts.cooked:
Hey everyone, I just signed up to post and respond on k the comments about American oak and Ridge wines. Eric Baugher here. I’ve got the 2013 CF Rousten in my cellar and have t opened it in a long time to taste. After I left Ridge, went to Napa for two vintages, my palate was almost wrecked by the extreme over use of new French oak. I loved a lot of those barrels that I was using, but the flavors were so intense. Now that I’m working on my own brand, I’ve gone back to mostly American oak and the trick with it is using the best cooperage that has a great source of stave wood that’s been properly seasoned. The art of barrel making is fascinating and subtle changes to time and temperature can influence how barrel impacts the wines. With 31 years of experience making wine and trying all the different cooperages, my own brand has narrowed down who makes the best barrel in America. Although, I’m still a firm believer in new American oak for Bordeaux varietals, the barrel must integrate and not overly dominate the aromas or taste. The best way to achieve that is by tempering the oak prior to filling with wine for the first time. Many winemakers don’t temper barrels as that’s lost flavor, but I do and that first extract using steam and hot water making a strong oak tea, that tea goes down the drain. Then I fill barrels with wine that’s still actively fermenting and filling with the heavy lees. Fermentation in barrel with heavy lees (creates thick coating barrier between the one and wood) helps with oak extraction control and integration. This was something we learned to start doing at Ridge with all the Monte Bello barrels. In theory all new barrels came in and at least got a good steaming and hot water rinse, but when 600-1200 new barrels arrived at harvest time finding time to do that was almost impossible. We also tried moving our barrel delivery dates to earlier in the year, but that was hard since most the cellar crew took summer off to head to Mexico to spend time at their homes there. We also had nasty wood boring beetles that were very active in summertime. So all this to say, we were mostly stuck with harvest time barrel receiving and tried to steam and soak as best we could to prep for filing with new vintages. The Historic Vineyard Series wines of Monte Bello, this CF part of, all received new oak as they were generally very concentrated wines that could absorb that much oak. Perhaps this CF should have gone into some once filled ex-MB barrels from 2011. I need to taste and see what I think now that so much time has passed. I no longer have the “house” palate and can clearly see if the oak is too dominant. I did feel that when I tasted 12 and 13 Monte Bello a month ago. I think maybe my palate has become so much more sensitive to oak now that I am making a small amount of wines under my label and careful about the oak integration and harmony of vineyard and grape variety showing.
As for whether we used too much new oak at Ridge, I feel strong that our use was appropriate for the varietals, vintage characteristics and the one style. Barrel sourcing became a much more important aspect of my job, just like buying corks, anything that impacted the flavors of the wine required careful experimentation-blind tasting trials. Whether or not they kept my records, there is a treasure trove of notes on all these tastings.

posts.name: Eric Baugher
posts.cooked:
I do believe we were becoming much more skillful at our barrel cooperage selection over my time at Ridge. When I began in 1994, we had two major cooperages supplying easily 90% of our barrels-half being ozark oak from Independent Stave Company (which became World Cooperage) and Bluegrass Cooperage from West Virginia. They were all barrels made in the bourbon barrel shape, steam bent and toasted (not charred.) Certain parts of the ozark forest made for stronger planky oak/dill flavors and since Independent Stave Co. marked our barrels from these micro-regions of the Ozark, we could learn about which barrels integrated well and which ones didn’t so that the next year’s orders would be refined based on taste. Bluegrass made either Minnesota (Stagmire Mill) or West Virginia oak barrels only. We found Minnesota oak was more tannic, less sweet and aromatic. I believe this was due to its northern latitude (short summers and long winters) and that changed the composition of the wood. West Virginia was my favorite. I think Paul’s, too. Unfortunately, the cooperage was taken over by Brown Foreman and they converted everything to actual boubon barrel production, no longer wine casks. Suddenly we lost one of our favorite barrels around 1998. We had to find replacements in that 53 gallon format, which was so unique that hardly any cooperages produced them for wine. The regular format cask for wine in is the 225L Bordeaux or the 228L Burgundy. Our barrels were the 200L (53 gal) classic american oak bourbon barrel for wine (remember these are not charred, but toasted.) We talked to Radoux about making us 53 gallons, we trialed Mendocinco Cooperage version of 53 gal, and struggled with our only remaining supplier Independent Stave. Once they became World Cooperage, the quality started to slip. They no longer separated the micro-regions of Ozark and good/bad were mixed at the cooperage. So from 1998-2000 it was a real struggle to find a successful replacement to Bluegrass Cooperage. Finally, 2000 came along and I was able to talk to another cooperage based in Lebanon, Kentucky. They were willing to make us 200L format bourbon barrels using their locally sourced white oak. We gave it a go, doing our standard MB, Chard, and Zin oak trials. Filling three barrels of each oak type, using a small tank and quarterly racking and returning so as to preserve the oak flavors of that barrel while clarifying the wine. At the end of a year, we could blind taste all the different barrels used and see which ones had the best integration. You see, at the end of the day, we did not want to make wines that reflected the art of barrel making, but instead use the barrels to complement the wines. Tempering the barrels was crucial to achieving this integration. Yes, it could be very difficult to assure that the barrels were properly steamed, hot water soaked an heavy oak tea dumped to the drain. I couldn’t be there at the steam/wash station for every single barrel and really counted on my cellar crew to get that work done. I’m sure the crew didn’t report when the steamer stopped, or the boiler cut out and was not producing hot water. Now, on my own project, and also when I was in Napa Valley, I was/am doing all that work personally. If done correctly, the results are awesome for the wine. I don’t dare say who or why, but at Ridge there was an aversion to spending labor and resources to do this job of tempering the new oak barrels and as such, it wasn’t being done. I also have a feeling that at Monte Bello this has likely changed, too. Maybe they have countered that by cutting back on how much new oak goes into the wines? Barrels were a major cost to the business and are only getting more expensive. When I began buying barrels for Ridge, those excellent Bluegrass barrels were about $150 in 1996. The barrels I’m buying for my wines this year (in the 53 gallon format) are $680. In the 225L format they $720, and if in french oak about $1400. Then on top of that the labor involved in barrel aging wines is significant. Quarterly racking, topping off losses, and keeping the barrels maintained while empty in between vintages. It’s a huge amount of work to use barrels for winemaking, but the benefit is in the wonderful aroma, flavors, and texture they can provide a wine. That can’t be achieved using some of the nasty tricks I learned in my two years in Napa Valley (oak on a rope, liquid oak -yuck)

I will dig out a bottle of the 2013 Rousten CF and see if its still showing dominant oak. It’s possible that it is, my last impression of the 2012 and 2013 MB was that they showed a lot of american oak. The Rousten CF vines at that point were beginning to express some symptoms of disease (red blotch) and the drought was starting to make the vines weaker. We did get full ripeness in 2013, but the vines could have begun to struggle on flavor and phenolic ripeness. Eventually the vines were ripped out and replanted. The new vines were not producing by the time I left Ridge.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Jonathan_Loesberg:

Obviously, oak is a controversial subject. To my taste, a wine that tastes of new oak won’t lose that flavor when it ages, though its place in the flavor profile may change. I may be wrong about that with some wines and I certainly haven’t tasted as many wines, both young and old, as some here have, but that is my experience.

Jonathan,

Oak is a subject I have mixed feelings about. I generally do not like wines that are marked by new oak. But, a few years ago some friends and drank several bottles of MB together and you could tell the oak integrating into the wine. By the time I got back to the 1999, I did not really notice oak, just great mature fruit.

Also, Jacky Truchot, my favorite producer, needed to buy some new oak barrels each year to replace some really old barrels. The new oak was used for one of his two grand crus - his Charmes Chambertin. I can remember tasting a range of 2001 and 2002 Truchot wines at a local store when the wines were young. The Charmes stood out like a sore thumb compared to his other grand cru (Clos de la Roche) and his various premier crus. Did not really taste like a Truchot wine. But, when the Charmes Chambertin matured (I had this from friends as I bought his other wines and not the Charmes Chambertin) it tasted like Truchot and one really could not tell that it was made differently from his other wines.

So, I have mixed emotions. I know that new oak can integrate and the wine can be fine, but it seems risky and I tend to shy away from wines with too much new oak unless I have a long history with the producer.

And, reading Eric discussing the cost of new barrels and the difficulties purchasing the perfect oak barrels, I have to ask, why age wine in 100% new oak? I guess I can understand it with wines where the oak masks a lack of fruit, but that certainly is not the case with MB. Seems like a needless and expensive risk to me. What am I missing?

posts.name: Eric Baugher
posts.cooked:
Hi Howard,
I was fortunate enough to enjoy many of those early vintage zinfandels. The library contained so many of those great wines and we had to continuously taste through and assess their quality. Funny thing about the resurrected Coast Range of the late 90’s was that it became the place were we 86’d those really faded, past their prime wines. Donn Reisen did a massive clear out of all the old stuff that never sold, collecting dust in the old cellar at the tasting room. Those were all sent to Paul and I to taste and sort through. All the funky, faded, oxidized wines, leaky corks, ullage were all opened and carefully dumped to tank. They all got combined with some declassified cabs from 1998 and bottled as NV Coast Range. I know the Coast Range 1,2,3 of the early 70’s were pretty good stuff. I had tasted those and somehow I recall them being blends of San Luis Obispo vineyards besides the Dusi Ranch. I’m not totally recalling that fact so it would be interesting if you recall the composition of those wines.

Anyway onto the main topic, Monte Bello, you have no fear that the 2012 or 2013 are fading or need to be sold. They are as I stated on the label, fifty+ year vintages. I think that’s probably why the wines still taste so young and oaky to me. Although, I have to tell you a funny story about the 2012 MB in a blind tasting with 150 wine professional tasting to do the Judgement of Paris 40th anniversary tasting in Las Vegas in 2016. I attended and represented Ridge. I was on the winemaker panel. We used the same scoring system at the 1976 tasting. The original wines in our younger vintages were present. In the case of MB, it was 2012-which was only in bottle for two years. I was tasting so hard and intensely, trying to sniff out MB, american oak, all the telltale signs of great vintage young MB. I thought I had it pinned on one glass. Tasted and scored it high, but there was another glass that was just a sliver slightly different and extremely difficult to decipher its personality and terroir. The Napa wines all tasted as expected for Napa and were easily identified. My scores reflected my opinion of that region and style. In the end, I highly rated all the Bordeaux wines and in what I thought was MB turned out to be Ch. Leoville Las-Cases 2010 and my number two wine was 2012MB. I was stunned, and I couldn’t believe how I could not spot american oak that day. Of course , the group score (combining all winemakers and wine professionals) was first place for 2012 Monte Bello. The next wine, by a big point gap, was 2010 Leoville Las-Cases. Then it went down from there and I can’t recall where it went and if CA vs Bx had any meaningful influence on consumer opinions as the original tasting of 1976. By the 40th anniversary the horse had been beaten, times were vastly different with the Chateau investing in winery upgrades. Now it feels that Bordeaux is chasing CA style, CA is chasing Bordeaux-in pursuit of balance (maybe not Napa or Paso, but perhaps other appellations.)

So all this to say, by no means sell those great vintages, drink them up and enjoy them! That was what they were made for. I’m doing the same with all mine in my collection. I would never dare think of selling them, they are my fond memories locked up in those bottles.

Cheers,
Eric

posts.cooked:
Hi William, it has been quite a long time since seeing each other and tasting wines. I remember the great dinner and wines we had at your place in Oxford. What about the time up at Ridge Monte Bello in February 2014, wasn’t that the first visit? The intense storm that was raging outside while we toured the winery and tasted, making leaving the winery a frightful time navigating the narrow road down the mountain.
Hopefully, we can connect in the near future. I’d love to show you what I’ve been working on down here in SC. Matthew Luczy visited me last week and got a chance to see and taste.

posts.name: Eric Baugher
posts.cooked:
Yes, the half barrel that was stocked with the remnants of older library wines that we tasted and judged good to sell and with a great price. There was always something affordable and delicious in the half-barrel. That’s gone away a long time ago, once the supply of older bottlings dried up.

Monte Bello up to 1992, was not in 100% new oak. I believe it was 1993 (a cool vintage at MB) that the shift to 100% took place. The earlier vintages 60’s-70’s had very little new oak, or if they did it was maybe 25%. They in fact, imported wine in french oak casks to bottle and sell wine through the tasting room. They would ask the Chateau to ship the wine in their best/youngest barrels so that once emptied they could re-use the barrels for MB, GY, Dusi that were being made at the time. I think it was '67 when Ridge had its importer license to bring in barrels that way. Forget what the wine was, but it was from Bordeaux.
In my time at Ridge, we mostly stuck to 100% new oak (95% american, 5% french.) It varied in that split and would occasionally include some eastern european oak. I know I had a couple of vintages where I backed off on new oak, we experimented with using once filled ex-MB barrels to see how that would work. As I recall, that provide the texture and sweetness to the mid-palate that we get from new oak. Over time, we became more confident in new oak use along with making sure barrels were well-tempered prior to filling. Up until 1997, the steam and wash method was combined with filling the Monte Bello barrels with Paso Robles Zin, letting the wine rest there for a few weeks to soak in the first extract. We’d then pull the wine out of barrel, clean them well, send the PR zin to the oldest barrels to age and integrate all that new oak. The MB barrels were then ready to be filled with the lots from Monte Bello. We stopped doing this method after all our experiments of 1997, trialing barrel malolactic of the MB lots directly in their new barrels. That worked far better with integration and it kept the barrels safe from spoilage potentials coming from the Paso Robles Zinfandel.
